nodes.title: Keep escape rules in one map
nodes.why: A single lookup object makes it easy to see and update all escape mappings.
nodes.alternative: Write separate `replace` calls for each character.
nodes.tradeoff: Cleaner maintenance, with a little extra setup code.

nodes.title: Convert input to string first
nodes.why: This safely handles non-string inputs like numbers or `null` without crashing.
nodes.alternative: Require callers to always pass strings.
nodes.tradeoff: More robust function behavior, but it may hide input type issues upstream.

nodes.title: Escape all target characters in one pass
nodes.why: One regex plus callback keeps replacement logic compact and consistent.
nodes.tradeoff: Efficient and centralized, though regex syntax can feel less familiar to beginners.

nodes.title: Start by coercing input to string
nodes.why: It guarantees the replace chain works even when input is not already a string.
nodes.alternative: Throw an error when input is not a string.
nodes.tradeoff: More forgiving behavior, but less strict input validation.

nodes.title: Replace `&` before other characters
nodes.why: Escaping ampersands first prevents double-escaping entities created later.
nodes.tradeoff: Correct output ordering, but this sequence requirement is easy to forget.

nodes.title: Use a readable replacement chain
nodes.why: Each line documents one escape rule clearly, which is easy to scan quickly.
nodes.alternative: Use a map + callback regex approach.
nodes.tradeoff: Straightforward to read, but longer and slightly repetitive.
